critical_findings.detail: User input directly interpolated via f-string into SQL query: f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '{username}'"
critical_findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?', (username,))
critical_findings.cross_ref: Same file has 3 additional vulnerabilities (CRITICAL + HIGH + MEDIUM)

critical_findings.detail: User input passed to subprocess.call() with shell=True: subprocess.call(f"echo {cmd}", shell=True)
critical_findings.recommendation: Use subprocess.run(['echo', cmd]) without shell=True, or validate input with strict whitelist
critical_findings.cross_ref: Same file demonstrates systemic insecure coding pattern

critical_findings.detail: User input directly concatenated into SQL query: cursor.execute(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
critical_findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries with placeholders: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', (user_id,))
critical_findings.cross_ref: File also has syntax error (line 42), command injection (line 32), and yaml.load (line 39)

critical_findings.detail: User input passed directly to os.system(): os.system(f"convert {user_filename} output.png")
critical_findings.recommendation: Use subprocess.run(['convert', user_filename, 'output.png']) or validate filename against strict pattern
critical_findings.cross_ref: Same file as SQL injection — suggests need for secure coding training

critical_findings.detail: Code snippet in report references subprocess.call(f"echo {cmd}", shell=True) — stored vulnerability reference
critical_findings.recommendation: N/A — this is a report referencing a pre-existing vulnerability, not live code

critical_findings.detail: Regex pattern string containing shell backtick pattern matched as potential injection: (r"`.*\$", ...) — this is a regex definition, not executable code
critical_findings.recommendation: False positive — this is a regex pattern string in the scanner itself, not executable code. Consider adding exclude pattern or comment annotation.

code_health_assessment.label: CRITICAL
code_health_assessment.top_issues: 8 critical (SQL injection, command injection), 8 high (pickle, secrets), 2 medium (yaml.load, hardcoded credentials), massive type hint deficits across tools/
code_health_assessment.note: Code health score is heavily dragged down by 484 low-severity type-hint findings. The real risk is concentrated in 2 files (both test targets) that together contain 6 CRITICAL vulnerabilities.

recommendations: Address all CRITICAL SQL injection findings first — user input should NEVER be interpolated into SQL queries without parameterization
Replace all subprocess.call(..., shell=True) patterns with argument-based invocation
Replace pickle.load() with JSON deserialization or authenticated pickle protocols in all source files
Replace yaml.load() with yaml.safe_load() everywhere — yaml.load() accepts arbitrary Python objects
Move hardcoded secrets (DB_PASSWORD, API keys) to environment variables or a secrets manager
Implement a pre-commit hook with Bandit or similar SAST tool to catch injection vulnerabilities before they reach the codebase
Add project-wide type annotations — the codebase has 211 functions without return types and 168 with partial types, making the code hard to maintain and refactor safely
Clean up 36 unused imports across tools/ — dead code increases cognitive load and masks real issues

Consider that the 50 files scanned represent the full workspace — the production code is in mas-engineer/ where the main concern is type hint hygiene (massive gap) but no security vulnerabilities detected
Run a focused security review on any code that accepts external input before it enters production
